label: Mexico City
sub: Federal capital — seat of Sheinbaum government

label: Guadalajara
sub: Jalisco — CJNG heartland, World Cup host city, 2,500 troops deployed

label: Tapalpa
sub: El Mencho killed here Feb 22, 2026 — defining event of current crisis

label: Culiacán
sub: Sinaloa — Los Chapitos vs. Los Mayos civil war ongoing

label: Salamanca
sub: Guanajuato — Cecilia García Ramblas abducted, confirmed killed Mar 31

label: Monterrey
sub: Nuevo León — World Cup host city, major economic hub

label: Tijuana
sub: Baja California — major fentanyl trafficking corridor to US

label: Nuevo Laredo
sub: Tamaulipas — CDN hub; Casino Centenario sanctioned Apr 14 (fentanyl stash house). Key USMCA trade corridor.

label: Ciudad Juárez
sub: Chihuahua — Sinaloa plaza boss 'El Pato' arrested Apr 4, 2026 in first FTO counterterrorism op

label: Acacoyagua
sub: Chiapas — Sinaloa Mayos kidnapping cell arrested Apr 3, 5 captives freed

label: San Pedro de las Colonias
sub: Coahuila — Patrocinio ejido: Mexico's largest extermination site, 70 graves, 1.5 tons remains

label: Tampico
sub: Tamaulipas — Diamante Casino & CAMSA sanctioned Apr 14 as CDN money laundering fronts (US Treasury OFAC)

label: Teotihuacan
sub: Estado de México — Gunman kills 1 Canadian tourist, injures 13 internationals at pyramid site Apr 20

label: Morelos, Chihuahua
sub: Chihuahua — 6 synthetic drug labs raided Apr 18; 2 US Embassy officers + 2 Mexican AEI officers killed in convoy crash returning from raid

label: México-Tenochtitlán
sub: Founded 1325 on an island in Lake Texcoco; capital of the Aztec Empire and greatest city in the pre-Columbian Americas; fell to Spanish forces on August 13, 1521.

label: Veracruz
sub: Cortés landed here April 22, 1519, founding Villa Rica de la Vera Cruz. Today Mexico's largest port and gateway for 60% of seaborne cargo.

label: Dolores Hidalgo
sub: Where Padre Hidalgo rang the church bell on September 16, 1810, launching Mexico's independence war. The Grito de Dolores is re-enacted annually by the President from Mexico City.

label: Puebla
sub: Site of the May 5, 1862 Battle of Puebla where General Zaragoza repelled France's best army. Also the location of Cholula — where Cortés massacred thousands in 1519. World Cup host 2026.

label: Querétaro
sub: Site of the 1917 Constitution signing (February 5) and Maximilian's execution (June 19, 1867). Today a booming industrial and aerospace hub near Mexico City.

label: Oaxaca
sub: Birthplace of Benito Juárez (1806), Mexico's greatest 19th-century statesman. Site of Monte Albán — 2,500-year-old Zapotec capital. Center of indigenous rights movements and APPO 2006 commune.

label: Guanajuato
sub: Colonial silver capital — the Valenciana mine made New Spain the world's largest silver producer. The Alhóndiga de Granaditas (1810) was the site of the independence movement's first major massacre and Hidalgo's displayed severed head.

label: Morelia
sub: Capital of Michoacán; birthplace of José María Morelos. Colonial-era cathedral and historic center (UNESCO). Today a flashpoint for CJNG-Viagras cartel conflict.

label: San Cristóbal de las Casas
sub: Chiapas — seized by EZLN Zapatistas on January 1, 1994, the day NAFTA took effect. Still hosts autonomous indigenous communities with Zapatista governance. Tourism and indigenous crafts hub.

label: Iguala
sub: Guerrero — where 43 Ayotzinapa normalista students disappeared on September 26–27, 2014, in Mexico's most prominent human rights case. Guerreros Unidos territory. Plan de Iguala (1821) independence pact also signed here.

label: Acapulco
sub: Once Mexico's premier Pacific resort; now among the world's most violent cities with 111 homicides/100,000 in peak years. Los Ardillos, Los Rojos, and Guerreros Unidos cartel territory. Pacific terminus of Manila Galleon trade 1565–1815.

label: Chichén Itzá
sub: Major Maya city-state 800–1200 CE; UNESCO World Heritage since 1988; one of the New Seven Wonders of the World. Receives 2.5 million visitors annually — key to Yucatán tourism economy.

label: Palenque
sub: Classic Maya city-state 600–800 CE; UNESCO World Heritage since 1987. Home of K'inich Janaab' Pakal (reigned 615–683 CE), whose tomb was discovered 1952. Located near Tren Maya railway line opened by AMLO (2023).

label: Mérida
sub: Capital of Yucatán; colonial 'White City'; consistently ranked Mexico's safest major city. Gateway to Chichén Itzá and Yucatán archaeological zone. Booming tourism and expat economy. Terminus of Tren Maya.

label: Cancún
sub: Mexico's top international tourism destination — $8B+ annually. Developed from scratch after 1974 government resort project. Receives 12+ million tourists/year. Key Quintana Roo economic driver.

label: Hermosillo
sub: Sonora capital; Ford Motor Co. Hermosillo Stamping & Assembly plant (240,000 vehicles/yr). Nearshoring hub for US-Mexico auto supply chains. Near Cananea copper mine (1906 strike site).

label: Saltillo
sub: Coahuila capital; automotive cluster (Chrysler, GM, KIA) — Mexico's second-largest auto manufacturing hub. Key USMCA-qualifying production zone. Site of Armando Fuentes Aguirre 'Catón' political commentary.

label: Nogales
sub: Sonora-Arizona border crossing — major USMCA fresh produce corridor ($4B+ annual trade). Twin city with Nogales, Arizona. Sinaloa Cartel influence; high fentanyl seizure rate. Maquiladora hub.

label: Matamoros
sub: Tamaulipas — facing Brownsville, Texas. Gulf Cartel (CDG) and Scorpions sub-faction territory. Site of March 2023 kidnapping of 4 US citizens (2 killed) that triggered US FTO debate. USMCA auto parts hub.

label: Reynosa
sub: Tamaulipas — largest maquiladora city on the US-Mexico border; faces McAllen, TX. Gulf Cartel territory. Largest USMCA electronics and medical device export corridor. Population 700,000+.

label: Coatzacoalcos
sub: Veracruz — Gulf terminus of AMLO's Interoceanic Corridor (Corredor Interoceánico) linking the Gulf to the Pacific across the Isthmus of Tehuantepec. $2.3B infrastructure investment; inaugurated 2023.

label: Salina Cruz
sub: Oaxaca — Pacific terminus of the Interoceanic Corridor (Isthmus of Tehuantepec). PEMEX refinery. Planned special economic zones to attract manufacturing. Part of AMLO's strategy to redirect trade routes from Panama Canal.

label: Dos Bocas Refinery
sub: Tabasco — AMLO's $16B Olmeca refinery project, inaugurated July 2023. PEMEX's first new refinery in 40 years. Intended to process 340,000 b/d but producing far below capacity. Symbol of AMLO's energy re-nationalization.

label: Tula (Tollan)
sub: Hidalgo — capital of the Toltec civilization (900–1150 CE), predecessor of Aztec culture. Famous for Atlante warrior sculptures. Mythologized in Aztec memory as the original paradise of Quetzalcóatl. PEMEX refinery complex nearby.

label: Cholula
sub: Puebla — site of the world's largest pyramid by volume (Great Pyramid of Cholula). Cortés massacred thousands here in 1519; he built a church on top of the pyramid as a symbol of conquest. 365 Catholic churches in the municipality.

label: Celaya
sub: Guanajuato — CJNG vs. Santa Rosa de Lima Cartel battle zone; peaked at 100+ homicides/month in 2019–2020. Site of Tlahuelilpan pipeline explosion (Jan 2019, 137 deaths) in nearby Hidalgo — the human cost of fuel theft (huachicoleo).

label: Chilpancingo
sub: Guerrero capital — where Morelos convened the 1813 Congress of Chilpancingo and issued Sentimientos de la Nación. Today a flashpoint for Guerrero political violence; multiple governors and officials assassinated in the state.

label: Chihuahua
sub: Capital of Mexico's largest state; where Hidalgo was executed (1811) and Pancho Villa launched his División del Norte (1913). Today center of the CIA-Maru Campos diplomatic row (Apr 2026) and Sinaloa-CDN cartel contest.

label: San Luis Potosí
sub: Colonial silver city; where Madero wrote the Plan de San Luis Potosí (1910) that launched the Revolution. Also briefly served as Juárez's capital during the French Intervention. Major industrial hub for BMW and Nestle today.
